turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case No. 15짯5238, Nichols v. United States. Mr. Hansmeier.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Until three weeks ago, a sex offender who moved to a foreign country was not subject to any Federal registration requirement in the United States unless and until he returned to the United States. Below, the Tenth Circuit nonetheless held that it is a Federal crime for a sex offender who moves to a foreign country to fail to 짯짯 to report that move. The Tenth Circuit was wrong for three overarching reasons. First, SORNA's text does not include a requirement to unregister. Second, SORNA's statutory history reveals that if Congress wanted to include a requirement to unregister, it would have done so just as it did before SORNA's enactment. And finally, a requirement to unregister is unnecessary because all 56 jurisdictions require sex offenders to unregister. Failure to do so is a violation of the jurisdiction's law and not Federal law.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: That would be true of Kansas law in this case. It would have violated the Kansas law. Is that true?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: He had an 짯짯 he had a requirement to tell Kansas of his departure, correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: How does it work normally? Say a person under SORNA leaves Kansas and goes to Illinois and registers in Illinois. Is there any obligation, one, for him to register or deregister in Kansas, and does Illinois have an obligation to notify Kansas?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Correct. So what happens in practice is that SORNA sets minimum Federal standards. And those minimum Federal standards are supplemented by the States, the additional, more stringent requirements. One of those more stringent requirements is the obligation to unregister, and, yes, Kansas has that obligation. So in your hypothetical, Justice Ginsburg, that individual has to unregister in Kansas under State law, and when he gets to Illinois, that's the Federal requirement to tell Illinois 짯짯 to register, to update the registration in Illinois because that's the jurisdiction where he resides. And in Illinois under 16921(b)(3) will tell 짯짯 will tell Kansas of the 짯짯 of the move.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And this is in addition to the 짯짯 the other venues.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: If there are any, correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So it would be both Federal and a State requirement. But in one case, it's on the SORNA person and the other, it's the other State.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I think that's correct, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Under 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What sense does it make if you have Federal law that says we're going to add this 짯짯 these 짯짯 these sanctions when you violate the 짯짯 the State law? And in the one case, if you don't register, you're triggered and State law says, well, you've got to unregister too. But if you unregister 짯짯 if you don't unregister, that's not included under the Federal law? Why would 짯짯 why would Congress draft a law like that?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 I think they would have done that because 짯짯 and if you look at the legislative history, I think this is somewhat clear. What Congress was concerned about were missing sex offenders not at initial registration but when 짯짯 when the sex offender crossed State lines. So 짯짯 so by 짯짯 by requiring registration when you get to the jurisdiction, that 짯짯 that was the aim because that's when offenders were going missing. Now, the 짯짯 the departure jurisdiction would be a relationship between 짯짯 very much a relationship between the State and its citizens. And that 짯짯 that's played out if you think of a State sex offender who could live in Kansas. And if he were to have a Federal requirement to unregister in Kansas, that's actually not enforceable under SORNA because the travel has to come after or before he's 짯짯 he can't register. So if Mr. Nichols were a State sex offender and 짯짯 he's only committed an offense when he leaves Kansas. And that doesn't 짯짯 and that's a problem if we're saying that there's a Federal requirement to unregister in 짯짯 in the departure jurisdiction. Now, that may work for Federal offenders, but I think the idea is that there are Federalism concerns underlying SORNA. And those Federalism concerns come into play when an individual crosses State lines, and that's the minimum requirement in SORNA.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but there's no problem to require him to register, but all of a sudden there is a problem to require him to unregister?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, it's not a problem. It's just SORNA left it to the States.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: They left the unregistering to the States but not the registering?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Not a 짯짯 not the updating the registration in a new State, correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm curious. You responded to Justice Ginsburg that he did violate State law. Could the United States have asked for an extradition based on the State law violation? Is this a permanent hole or did they just pick the wrong law to seek extradition under?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, Justice Ginsburg's example was interstate travel.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no. I'm saying, they asked him to be 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: But you're talking 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 짯짯 returned from 짯짯 where had he fled to? Philippines.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Mr. Nichols moved 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Under the SORNA violation 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 짯짯 could they have asked for him to be returned under the State law violation?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: There's actually a provision in SORNA, I think it's 19 짯짯 16917, which requires Federal 짯짯 the marshals, Federal officials to assist the States in enforcing the registration requirement.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So is that a yes?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I think yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So really what we're talking about is that they asked for the extradition under the wrong law.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I think that's exactly right. And 짯짯 and Mr. Nichols was also on Federal supervised release, so there was a connection there that 짯짯 the connection to the Federal aspect of it was his 짯짯 was his supervision.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Did leave 짯짯 did leaving the country without notice violate the terms of his supervised release?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: It did. And he was 짯짯 his supervised release was revoked. He 짯짯 he received a ten짯month sentence for that. And then they still prosecuted him Federally, and he got another six짯month sentence.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Are you 짯짯 it's mentioned in one 짯짯 one of the briefs 짯짯 I don't have the reference to it 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: 짯짯 at hand. What would happen if he decides he's 짯짯 and this is all just within the United States, no foreign travel 짯짯 he decides that he wants to move to the West Coast, so he checks out of Kansas. But he's not sure where he wants to live, so he drives to Washington, Oregon, California, takes two weeks. What would the requirement for registration be there?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Right. Well, I think it gets a little confusing with the interstate travel because we don't really address it because there is a jurisdiction. There is an arrival jurisdiction. I think under our rule, what 16913(c) says is that you don't unregister. You 짯짯 you register in the arrival jurisdiction. Now, there may be 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But the arrival jurisdiction is not your residence yet. He doesn't know what it's going to be. He's just there two days. And he's going to go two days, and he's going to look around.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 I think SORNA doesn't do a good job of this. The definition of 짯짯 they define "resides" as the location of one's home or the location where one habitually lives. And so I do think that SORNA does not do a good job of this, but I'm not sure that that wasn't unintended. Because if you think about the 짯짯 the 짯짯 the window, the three짯business짯day window 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: 짯짯 that 짯짯 that actually creates a system where an individual is not on the registry for a certain amount of time. If you think of a change of name, which you have to report, if you have three business days to do that, there is going to be up to five days where that registry is inaccurate, and that's just the way it is. So I think, although we might not want to admit it or 짯짯 but the 짯짯 the reality is that SORNA actually creates these gaps by allowing offenders some period of time to register. And I think Congress did that because it knew the States could supplement SORNA. These are just minimum standards.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: You have a textual argument that I 짯짯 that I understand, but one of the arguments you mentioned at the outset was that an examination of the legislative history shows that Congress intended to produce the sort of result that was produced here, and I wonder if that's really a plausible argument. If you're correct, then two things happen; at least two things happen. One, Kansas retains on its record an inaccurate 짯짯 inaccurate information about the presence of a sex offender, which can have a lot of adverse consequences for a lot of people. I don't see why Congress would have wanted that to remain. And do you really think Congress was unconcerned about the United States exporting pedophiles to the Philippines or to other countries? Do you think that 짯짯 that they were not concerned about not only the human rights implications of that, but our relations with other countries?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well 짯짯 well, I think if you look at the legislative history, there 짯짯 there's nothing to indicate that 짯짯 that SORNA in 2006 had anything to do with international travel. And I think the new legislation is important in this regard, because whether 짯짯 I don't 짯짯 I don't know that I said "intended." If I said that, I didn't mean it. I think the 짯짯 the history of it shows more or less that in 1996, each State had a registration system. One year later, Congress required departure notification, required unregistration. By 2006, that had happened in all the jurisdictions. So I think what I mean by the "statutory history" is that that's 짯짯 that's an indication why there was no unregistration requirement put in SORNA, because it existed in all the jurisdictions.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, if I could then come back to Justice Ginsburg's hypothetical of someone like your client who moves from Kansas to Illinois, what, in the language of the statute, would prevent that person from going to the registry office in Kansas before departing and reporting there that he was moving to Illinois and his new address in Illinois would be whatever it would be? Why does that not fit under this 짯짯 under the language of the statute? Or would you agree that it does?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, I would agree that there's nothing that prevents him from doing that, and I would agree that he should do that under State law, but 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, if you 짯짯 if you agree to that, then I don't see why 짯짯 why there is a difference between that situation and the situation where the 짯짯 the person leaves Kansas and goes to the Philippines.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, what 짯짯 but I don't agree that it's a 짯짯 it's sufficient or a Federal requirement to do it before. That 짯짯 I think that's the distinction I would make 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Why would it not be 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: 짯짯 it's not a requirement.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Why would it not be sufficient under 42 U.S.C. 16913(c)?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, because that provision is still going to require 짯짯 and the government, I think, agrees with this. There is still a 짯짯 the 짯짯 the requirement is to report in the arrival jurisdiction.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, it says, "shall not, later than three business days after each change of residence, appear in person in at least one jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a) and inform that jurisdiction of the change of address." So prior to leaving, he is within the period of not later than what 짯짯 than three business days after the change of residence. And at that time when he goes to the registry office in Kansas, he is residing in Kansas. So he falls within subsection (a).

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, this is on 짯짯 the text is on page 11A of the blue book. I think 짯짯 I think what's 짯짯 the blue brief. I think what's important is 짯짯 is if you looked directly above subsection (c) to subsection (e), I think this is what you're really saying Congress did. Subsection (b) sets forth two subsections, the first of which begins with the word "before." The second of which begins with the phrase "not later than three business days after." So I think if Congress had meant to do something structurally similar in subsection (c), it would have done it, but it didn't. Instead, it just did "not later than three business days after." And 짯짯 and I think the change is a trigger, and I think that's 짯짯 that's key, and it's similar to subsection (b)(2) where the trigger is being sentenced. And 짯짯 and so that event has to happen, that occurrence has to happen before you report it. And so in subsection (c), until the change happens, you can't really report it and satisfy this provision.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But now, under current law, under the 2016 change 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: 짯짯 is it 짯짯 it's clear that anyone under SORNA who leaves the country 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: 짯짯 has to give notice 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: 짯짯 to the jurisdiction from which he departs.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Correct. And the statutory 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So they're talking about only the people who were 짯짯 were pre 짯짯 pre짯2016 people.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: We're talking about a very small subset, I assume, unless the Federal government were to go out and indict a bunch of people who have traveled prior to February 8th for this.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: What bearing on your argument does the last sentence at the bottom of 11(a), the last sentence of C have? That jurisdiction, in other words, the jurisdiction where he registered, shall immediately provide that information to all other jurisdictions in which the defendant is required to register. Here, there were none.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, correct. And 짯짯 and typically, there may not be. But that 짯짯 that language, I think, captures the defendant 짯짯 for instance, where we come from, a lot of people live in Kansas and work in Missouri. That 짯짯 that sentence would capture that situation where there is a 짯짯 or a 짯짯 an 짯짯 an employment jurisdiction that's different than a residence jurisdiction. Then the employment jurisdiction would notify the residence jurisdiction or vice versa.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: My question was, does 짯짯 does this have significant relevance to your argument here?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Does it help?

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I 짯짯 I don't think it 짯짯 I just think it's how it's 짯짯 I don't know that it helps. It's 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: There was a reference to a form that everyone under SORNA signs that acknowledges a requirement to register on commencement change, termination of residence, and give 21 days notice before traveling outside the United States. Nichols signed such a form, did he not?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: He did.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: And what you're saying is that that form has no operative effect, the fact that he didn't do what he 짯짯 what he undertook to do in the form is not a Federal offense.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, what I'm saying is that that's a State form. That's actually the State of Kansas. So SORNA is implemented by the States. So each State 짯짯 there's not some Federal form for sex offenders to sign. There are no Federal registration offices. There are no Federal officials who do these things. This is a local thing. A lot of it is county sheriffs. It would be a State form. Every State would have a different form. So what you are referring to, those are quotation summaries of Kansas law, and not Federal law, because if you're in Kansas, you have to 짯짯 you are required to register, to update your registration as required by Kansas law, which is much more in짯depth than Federal law. And so that's where that language comes from.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But it's the same register, that is 짯짯 you said there's no separate Federal entity. So all these requirements in SORNA would be satisfied by informing the State office. Is that 짯짯

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Well, I'm saying, in practice, that's what has to happen. There's no Federal person you can tell. You register with the State people.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I find interesting the new legislation. As I see it, it's getting away from where you reside or anything else. It just says if you're going to travel to a foreign place, you have to tell the United States, correct?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Is there anything in that 짯짯 in that law that requires the U.S. to tell the State the person's left 짯짯 that they've left?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I think there is a provision for that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I didn't 짯짯 I didn't have a complete chance to go through it.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I don't know that that provision made its way into the 짯짯 the government's supplemental brief. Yes. I think at (4)(a), if I'm reading this correctly, there's an implementation provision.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the problem at issue here has been completely resolved by new legislation?

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Oh, I think that's absolutely true. I think if there 짯짯 whether there was a gap or not, whether it was intended or not, the bottom line is that it's gone away. And there 짯짯 there's no reason to take an atextual interpretation of 16913(c). I 짯짯 I do want to mention something this Court said in Reynolds, because I think this Court is on the same path as us. Reynolds was the second SORNA case that the Court heard. And there was 짯짯 there's a point in Reynolds in which the Court is talking about the defendant, and it says 짯짯 it says that he was in prison, he was released, he registered in Missouri, but he moved to Pennsylvania. And it says "without updating his Missouri information," and then this Court puts in parentheses, "as Missouri law required and without registering in Pennsylvania." Then it goes on to talk about how the failure to register in Pennsylvania is a 16139(c) offense. That's our position. And I think this Court 짯짯 you said it in Reynolds. It had nothing to do with the holding. But I 짯짯 I do think that's 짯짯 that's in this Court's mind, at least. And 짯짯 and I do want to note that the government's reading defining the supplemental jurisdiction is one where the defendant appears as a resident on the 짯짯 on 짯짯 on a registry creates a really weird situation where Mr. Nichols, when he lived in the Philippines, because he still appeared on the Kansas registry, under SORNA, he still lives there. He still resides there. And that's just not right under the statute and the way SORNA defines "resides." And it's just not right as a matter of common sense. You don't reside in Kansas when you actually live in the Philippines. And with that, if there are no further questions, thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Gannon.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: SORNA required Petitioner to register and to keep his registration information current. This is not an unregistration requirement, but a requirement to keep his registration current in the jurisdictions where he was registered by timely giving notice of any change of residence to at least one jurisdiction involved. And we disagree with Petitioner. We think Kansas was a jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a) here, both because of the meaning of that phrase, which is more capacious than just taking down the phrase that appeared in subsection (a), and also because it's reinforced by Section 16921(b)(3).

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Is it your position that he was required to notify Kansas both that he was terminating his residence and that he was moving to the Philippines, or just that he was terminating his residence?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Here it's just that he's terminating his residence. He could have given information about where he was going.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But he was not required to?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: But that is 짯짯 that is not what he was being required to do here. He was being required to keep his registration information current by saying where he will 짯짯 where 짯짯 where he is residing, and he will 짯짯 he is no longer going to be residing here. The phrase that 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I 짯짯 I think your answer was clear, but I didn't understand it. Would it 짯짯 in your view 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: The 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: 짯짯 would it have been compliant with SORNA simply to say I am leaving Kansas, period?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That would have complied with his obligation to keep his registration information current with the jurisdiction involved, yes. The 짯짯 we do think that Section 16914(a)(3) allows him to report where he resides or will reside, and therefore, he can report future information, and therefore, the guidelines, promulgated by the Attorney General in 2008, says that normally when somebody's moving from one jurisdiction to another jurisdiction, he should tell Kansas, I am leaving Kansas and this is where I'm going next, so Kansas can give that other jurisdiction warning.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But the 짯짯 the Philippines is not a jurisdiction under the statute.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That's correct. And so what we think is key here is that Kansas is still a jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a). Congress didn't say a jurisdiction described in subsection (a); it said a jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a).

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, all right. How is that the case since subsection (a) refers to each jurisdiction where the offender resides, is an employee or a student? When he's in the Philippines, there's no such jurisdiction covered by SORNA where he resides, is an employee, or is a student.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That's correct, Mr. Chief Justice, but we think that that's not the only thing that is pulled in by the phrase, "jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a)." He is required to register while he's a registrant in Kansas. He's required to keep 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But how is that 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: At 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Where is under (a) something that says it's the jurisdiction where you used to be required to register?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: It's 짯짯 it's the part that says he has to keep the registration information current. And subsection (c) informs us what it means to keep the registration 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But where 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: 짯짯 information current. That's further down on the page. This is page 7A of the government's brief.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but he only has to keep the registration current if there is a jurisdiction where he 짯짯 covered jurisdiction where he resides, is an employee or is a student.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Otherwise that obligation is inoperative because there's no registration place in the Philippines.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well, we 짯짯 we disagree that Kansas is not a 짯짯 is not a jurisdiction involved. But even if it were not such a jurisdiction, subsection (c) requires him to give notice to at least one jurisdiction. And as Justice Alito has already explained 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: 짯짯 it is also the case here that he could have complied with that by giving the notice before he left Kansas 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, right.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: 짯짯 when he was in dispute 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But he doesn't have to comply with it until later than three business days after there's a change.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That's not 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS

turns.name: MR. GANNON

And so if he can't comply with it on business day one or business day two or business day three thereafter, then maybe he should have complied with it by informing Kansas before he left. The 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, maybe he should have. Maybe he should have. But he may also have thought, when it says at least one jurisdiction involved pursuant to subsection (a), and subsection (a) says where he resides, then maybe there's no jurisdiction involved under 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: But 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: 짯짯 under the 짯짯

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: It does say where he resides. But it also says he has to keep the registration information current there. And we think that Section 16921 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Current where?

turns.text: Right. What 짯짯 what sort of language did they use?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That their language does 짯짯 it specifically says, "He must reregister any change or termination of residence within three days to the registering law enforcement agency or agencies where last registered." So that's the 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But it says "where last registered."

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Where it specifically picks up and says, you have to go back to where you last registered, we think that goes beyond 짯짯 that's the way in which Kansas law goes beyond SORNA, because SORNA does not always require that. We think that in a situation like this, SORNA effectively required that, because there's no other place for him to have gone. But we also take Petitioner's concession that SORNA permits him to comply with the requirement to keep his registration current by going to the office in Kansas. And it's the same form. There's no doubt that it's possible for him to report the change in residence there. The form says, what is your address? And it has location start date and location end date, and those can be reported in advance or after the fact. Our position is just that when SORNA gets around to saying what it means to keep your registration information current, you have to do that in at least one place within three business days. And so we think that that means he could do it in Kansas even the day after he moved. If you disagree with that, we still think it also must mean at least, as Justice Alito was explaining, that he has to do it 짯짯 he has to comply with it by doing it while there still is one jurisdiction. There's no doubt that Kansas was a jurisdiction involved while he was still residing there.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Could I 짯짯 could I ask a question, Mr. Gannon, of absolutely no legal significance? Chalk it up to curiosity. Does the Federal government bring many of these prosecutions? Because I might have thought that when sex offenders leave the country permanently, the attitude might be like, good luck and good riddance?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 I take the point, Justice Kagan, but remember this is somebody who is not just a Federal sex offender. He's on Federal supervisory release. The notion that we have somebody on Federal supervisory release, we're supposed to be keeping track of him and making sure that he's not committing other crimes. If 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But you're not 짯짯 you're 짯짯 as I understand the requirement that even you say he's subject to, it's just a requirement that says he's leaving the State. It doesn't 짯짯 he's not required to tell you where he's going in the world. He could be going to the Philippines or any other country. So it doesn't really help us in terms of, you know, helping our allies deal with sex offenders. I'm just curious why it is that people 짯짯 why DOJ makes the decision to bring sex offenders back to this country in such circumstances.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well, as 짯짯 as I said, he was a Federal sex offender who was on Federal supervisory release who appeared to be on the cusp of committing additional Federal offenses in the Philippines, and so we brought him back. And we do think that there's a Federal interest in this case. But Section 2250 includes cases where somebody is also violating State law, because by definition, all of these registration requirements in SORNA much of the time are also going to be violations of State law. And 짯짯 and so in this instance, the problem is that the registry was inaccurate. It's supposed to say where he's living.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I am right, right, that when you say he had the responsibility to keep the registry accurate, that's just to say he had the responsibility to tell Kansas he was no longer in Kansas.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Even on your view, he had no other responsibilities.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That 짯짯 that's 짯짯 that's the applicable responsibility here. That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: In terms of 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: How 짯짯 how do we 짯짯 how do we know 짯짯 I'm sorry. Go ahead, please.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: In terms of Federal policy, hasn't Congress, by the enactment of the new statute and in other ways, evidenced a belief that the United States should not be exporting its pedophile problems to other countries?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Yes, Justice 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: And countries like the Philippines where the 짯짯 the Petitioner here went are known places that are attractive to sex tourists who go there to abuse young children and 짯짯 hasn't Congress evidenced a concern about this both for the human rights aspects of the problem and, also, I would think, for relations with the government of the Philippines?

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Absolutely, Justice Alito. And it 짯짯 it would have been a Federal offense for him to have engaged in 짯짯 in sexually elicit conduct in the Philippines.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Right. And I completely understand that, Mr. Gannon, but even your own interpretation doesn't get you the information 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I knew we should have paid that bill.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: My red light on the bench is still working.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: 짯짯 does not get you the information that you need in order to tell the Philippines, look, you have a sex offender in your midst.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: That's 짯짯 that's correct, Justice Kagan. The new statute does that as did the supplemental guidelines that the Attorney General adopted in 2011 under SORNA as additional information that was required. That's not what's at issue in this particular case.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What's so interesting is that regulation tells the States to do this.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It doesn't tell the sex offender that he's obligated to do it. It says the States must do it.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: It's 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm 짯짯 it's a little bit odd.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: It's true that the guidelines were addressed. Both the guidelines and the supplemental guidelines were addressed to implementing jurisdictions. They were articulating the Federal government's construction of the statute and 짯짯 and what is required.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So now a sex offender is supposed to understand what "involved" means and read a directive to jurisdictions in your regulation and say, ah, that includes me, too.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well, we 짯짯 we 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Jurisdiction is supposed to do this, but now I've got to do something more.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Well, but what the jurisdiction was told is that it has to inform sex offenders that they must do this, and that happened here. Kansas informed him that he had to provide notice of any termination or change in his residence. That's what was required by SORNA.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What makes it a State as opposed to a Federal crime? That's really what's at issue here.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: It 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I understand it's a crime.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The question is which kind of crime.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Yes. And in this instance, Petitioner acknowledges it's a State crime. It is also a Federal crime. Mr. Chief Justice, May I finish answering?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Briefly.

turns.name: MR. GANNON
turns.text: Under Section 2250, because he's a Federal sexual offender, there doesn't even need to be independent travel. It's the violation of the SORNA requirements that makes it a Federal offense.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Hansmeier, you have 11 minutes remaining.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Sorry, Mr. Chief Justice. I don't think I actually reserved time for rebuttal, so thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: I forgot to say that, but thank you. I would 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: MR. HANSMEIER
turns.text: Justice Breyer, if I can 짯짯 I'll 짯짯 I'll be brief. On the guidelines 짯짯 this is actually a direct quote 짯짯 the 짯짯 the 짯짯 according to the Department of Justice, "A jurisdiction may require that changes in registration information be reported by registrants on a more stringent basis than the SORNA minimum standards. For example, requiring that changes of residence be reported before the sex offender moves rather than within three business days following the move." I mean, that's our position. I don't 짯짯 there are other portions in 짯짯 in the guidelines that direct jurisdictions to do this. But that 짯짯 but 짯짯 but what the 짯짯 what the guidelines say right there is 짯짯 is exactly what we say, that it 짯짯 it is a more stringent requirement. A few other points on the word "current." I think if you 짯짯 if you look in the 짯짯 in the 짯짯 there's a provision on in짯person verifications where there is requirements for a current photograph. That word clearly doesn't mean a future. And I think that's important to understand, that when you're talking about current information, it's not future information. I think Justice Kagan mentioned that a few times. On 1621 짯짯 16921(b)(3), again, that's 짯짯 that's language directed towards the jurisdictions. That's not a requirement for the offender, and that's how the former jurisdiction would be informed of the change. And if there are no other questions.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
